title: Slow-Cooked Pizza Sauce

overview.description: A rich, deeply flavoured pizza sauce made from slowly cooked tomatoes, onions, garlic and herbs.

method: Heat the olive oil in a large heavy-based saucepan over low to medium heat.
Cook gently for 15–20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until very soft and lightly golden.
Add the garlic and cook for 1–2 minutes.
Cook for 3–5 minutes, stirring regularly, until the purée darkens slightly.
Add the chopped tomatoes, oregano, dried basil and black pepper.
Cook uncovered for 1½–2 hours, stirring occasionally.
If the sauce begins to catch, reduce the heat and add a small splash of water.
The sauce should become thick, rich and concentrated.
Add the red wine vinegar during the final 10 minutes of cooking.
Stir through fresh basil if using.
Blend until smooth, or leave slightly textured according to preference.

learning.did-you-know: Pizza sauce is often used in relatively small quantities, so concentrating the tomatoes helps the flavour remain strong after the sauce is spread thinly across the dough.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How slow cooking develops sweetness and depth in tomatoes and onions and how to reduce a sauce to the correct thickness for pizza.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A batch-friendly sauce designed specifically for homemade pizzas, using pantry ingredients and freezing well in convenient portions.
chapman-tips: Cook the onions slowly; rushing this stage reduces the depth of flavour.
Cook the tomato purée before adding the tomatoes to develop a deeper flavour.
The sauce should be thicker than a normal pasta sauce.
Don't spread too much sauce on the pizza or the base may become soggy.
A little red wine vinegar helps balance the sweetness of the long-cooked tomatoes.
Blend the sauce if you prefer a smooth takeaway-style finish.
Freeze in small portions so you only defrost what you need.
Cool the sauce completely before freezing.
The sauce can be used straight from frozen after thawing in the refrigerator.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes very well. Freeze in individual pizza-sized portions or in larger containers for multiple pizzas.
notes.leftovers: Use in pasta sauces, meatballs, lasagne, shakshuka or as a dipping sauce.
